buggy_code:
def compute_signals(prices, lookback=20, results=[]):
    """
    Compute signals and accumulate in results list.
    BUG: mutable default argument persists across calls.
    """
    signal = prices.rolling(lookback).mean().iloc[-1]
    results.append(signal)
    return results

# First call
signals_a = compute_signals(prices_a)

# Second call — results already contains signals_a!
signals_b = compute_signals(prices_b)
clean_code:
def compute_signals(prices, lookback=20, results=None):
    if results is None:
        results = []
    signal = prices.rolling(lookback).mean().iloc[-1]
    results.append(signal)
    return results

signals_a = compute_signals(prices_a)
signals_b = compute_signals(prices_b)
description: Mutable default argument (list) persists state across function calls.
explanation: In Python, mutable default arguments are created once and reused. The results=[] list accumulates values from ALL previous calls. signals_b will contain both signals_a values AND signals_b values. In a backtesting context, this causes signal contamination across different assets or time periods.
suggested_fix: Use None as default and create a new list inside the function if not provided.

issues.title: Mutable default argument causes state contamination across calls
